title: Cat Vomiting Fresh Blood: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is vomiting fresh blood, it is a medical emergency. Learn the causes, urgency levels, and next steps to take for your pet's safety.
content_html:
TL;DR: Seeing fresh, bright red blood in your cat's vomit is a critical medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent life-threatening complications or shock.

What does it mean if my cat is vomiting fresh, red blood?

Vomiting fresh, bright red blood is medically known as hematemesis. This condition indicates active bleeding within the upper gastrointestinal tract, which includes the mouth, esophagus, or stomach. Unlike digested blood, which may look like dark coffee grounds, fresh blood suggests a recent or ongoing injury that requires immediate attention.

Is it a medical emergency if my cat is vomiting blood?

  • Urgency Level: High. Seeing fresh blood in your cat's vomit is a critical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention.
  • Potential causes include severe underlying issues such as the ingestion of toxins (like rodenticides), internal trauma, foreign body obstructions, or perforated ulcers.
  • Because cats often hide their pain and can deteriorate quickly, you must contact an emergency veterinarian immediately to prevent shock or fatal blood loss.

How can taking a photo of the vomit help the veterinary team?

  • A quick, clear photo provides a visual record that allows the vet to assess the volume and concentration of the blood.
  • This information helps the team prioritize your cat's care the moment you arrive at the clinic.
  • Photos provide essential diagnostic clues that may be lost once the area has been cleaned.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In emergency triage, profuse vomiting warrants immediate transfer of the cat to the treatment area. Vomiting of fresh blood (frank blood) suggests a bleeding lesion such as a gastric ulcer or neoplasm. Important parameters to evaluate during triage include mucous membrane color (pale or white indicating anemia or shock), capillary refill time (greater than 2 seconds indicating poor perfusion), heart rate (tachycardia in cats, >220 bpm), pulse rate and quality (weak or absent), and level of consciousness (depressed or obtunded).

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Surgery, Cardiology, Pulmonology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1660)

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if my cat gets better?
faqs.answer: No. Fresh blood indicates active internal bleeding or severe tissue damage. Waiting can lead to a life-threatening decline in your cat's condition.

faqs.question: Should I give my cat water if they are vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: Do not give your cat food, water, or any home remedies. Keeping their stomach empty is safer for potential emergency surgery or diagnostic procedures.

faqs.question: Could a small amount of blood just be from a scratch in the throat?
faqs.answer: While minor irritation can cause slight streaking, it is impossible to determine the severity without a professional exam. Always treat fresh blood as a high-priority emergency.
